title: Nehemiah 10
subtitle: The Sealed Covenant

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Ezra Reads the Law

heb.paragraph: Eighty-four names seal the document: Nehemiah first, then priests, Levites, and lay leaders. The naming is the commitment. Each signature binds a family.

refs.note: Joshua’s covenant with witnesses — the same legal pattern.
The Sinai covenant ceremony where Moses reads the law and the people respond 'we will do everything the LORD has said' — the prototype for Nehemiah 10's covenant renewal with its communal oath.
Josiah's covenant renewal — the last major pre-exilic covenant ceremony, which Nehemiah's post-exilic renewal parallels in structure and theological significance.

notes.note: Nehemiah signs first as governor. Political authority backs religious commitment. Church and state align in covenant purpose.
Twenty-one priests, seventeen Levites, forty-four lay leaders. The three tiers of community leadership are all represented. No class exempts itself.

Calvin: "The governor signed first. Leaders must lead in commitment, not merely in command.". Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes "on the sealed document" (ʿal-heḥātûm) indicates a clay-sealed or wax-sealed legal instrument. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

williamson.source: H.G.M. Williamson, Ezra, Nehemiah, WBC (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Williamson compares the signatory list with names from chapters 3 and 12, finding significant overlap. The same families who built the wall now seal the covenant. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Ezra and Nehemiah, TOTC (1979) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: "Eighty-four signatures. Each name is a promise. The covenant is not abstract but personal — bound to named individuals who can be held accountable.". Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

hist.context: The list serves a legal function: these are the guarantors. If the covenant is broken, these families bear responsibility. Public naming creates public accountability.

heb.paragraph: "We will not neglect the house of our God" (v.39). The covenant’s final sentence and its summary. Everything else — intermarriage, Sabbath, tithes, wood offering — serves this one principle.

refs.note: The intermarriage prohibition the covenant reaffirms.
The Sabbath and sabbatical year the covenant revives.

notes.note: No intermarriage — the issue from Ezra 9–10 is formally prohibited. The community draws a line it failed to hold before.
Sabbath enforcement: "We will not buy from foreign merchants on the Sabbath." Economic activity as covenant faithfulness. What you buy and when you buy it is a spiritual act.
A self-imposed temple tax of one-third shekel. The Torah prescribes half a shekel (Exod 30:13); the community adjusts downward because of poverty. Faithfulness within means.
"We will not neglect the house of our God." The covenant’s last word. Everything else flows from and toward this: the temple as the centre of covenant life.

Calvin: "We will not neglect. The simplest and most necessary of all vows. Neglect, not rebellion, is the church’s constant danger.". Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET discusses the one-third shekel as an adaptation from the Exod 30:13 half-shekel, likely reflecting economic hardship in the post-exilic community. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

williamson.source: H.G.M. Williamson, Ezra, Nehemiah, WBC (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Williamson notes the Sabbath commercial prohibition goes beyond Torah’s work-rest framework. The community is legislating against economic pressure to trade on the Sabbath — a specific post-exilic challenge. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Ezra and Nehemiah, TOTC (1979) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: "One-third shekel instead of half. They were too poor for the full amount but too faithful to give nothing. Grace adjusts the standard to the capacity."
Kidner: "The final sentence is the covenant in miniature. Not neglect. The opposite of faithfulness is not hostility but inattention."

hist.context: The commitments are specific, not vague: no intermarriage (v.30), Sabbath observance including refusing to buy from foreigners on the Sabbath (v.31), temple tax (v.32), wood offering (v.34), firstfruits (vv.35–37), tithes (vv.37–38). Each addresses a failure documented earlier in the book. The covenant is not aspirational but corrective.

ppl.name: Nehemiah
ppl.text: Nehemiah is a key figure in The Sealed Covenant.

ppl.name: Ezra
ppl.text: No intermarriage — the issue from Ezra 9–10 is formally prohibited…

Zedekiah,ESV“On the seals are the names of Nehemiah the governor, the son of Hacaliah, Zedekiah,

rec.text: "Nehemiah signs first as governor. Political authority backs religious commitment. Church and state align in covenant purpose."

rows.label: The Names — 10:1–27
rows.text: Eighty-four signatures. Each name a promise, each promise binding.

rows.label: The Commitments — 10:28–39
rows.text: Specific, corrective, within means. "We will not neglect the house of our God."

hebtext.note: Eighty-four names seal the document: Nehemiah first, then priests, Levites, and lay leaders. The naming is the commitment. Each signature binds a family.
לא נַעֲזֹב (lōʾ na'azōv) = we will not neglect

thread.text: Joshua’s covenant with witnesses — the same legal pattern.
The intermarriage prohibition the covenant reaffirms.
The Sabbath and sabbatical year the covenant revives.

themes.note: Covenant at 10 again: the chapter IS the covenant. Holiness is high — every commitment targets boundary-keeping.

coaching.tip: The community binds itself by written covenant — specifying intermarriage prohibitions, Sabbath observance, temple tax, wood offering, firstfruits, and tithes. The covenant is remarkably specific. Vague commitments produce vague obedience. Nehemiah understands that reform requires concrete, measurable pledges.

prayer_prompt: The sealed covenant committed the people to specific practices of faithfulness. Consider asking God about the specific commitments that would mark your renewed obedience.
